tasks.title: Linux and cross-platform distribution
tasks.description:
Linux binaries don't need code signing per se, but proper distribution channels need their own packaging and trust mechanisms. Musl builds and SHA-256 checksums are done via CI. Remaining: GPG signatures, Homebrew tap, crates.io, Linux package repos.

Tracking: GitHub issue https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/issues/3 (broader scope — Homebrew, crates.io, AUR, Scoop, WinGet, npm).

subtasks.title: Build static Linux binaries (musl)
Generate SHA-256 checksums and GPG signatures
subtasks.description: SHA-256 checksums are generated in CI. GPG signing of the checksums file is not yet implemented.

subtasks.title: Create Homebrew tap
Publish to crates.io
Evaluate APT/RPM/AUR packaging

tasks.title: Add coverage exclusion annotations to untestable code
tasks.description: Rust's #[coverage(off)] attribute is still unstable (tracking issue rust-lang/rust#134749). Once stabilized on stable Rust, annotate the ~129 lines of genuinely untestable code (main.rs I/O wiring, ring crypto internal errors, unreachable branches) so cargo llvm-cov reports accurate achievable coverage. See docs/test-coverage.md 'Theoretical Coverage Ceiling' section for the full list.

tasks.title: Design and generate branded app icons
tasks.description: Replace Tauri default icons with properly branded secrt icons. Need macOS .icns (1024px source), Windows .ico + Store PNGs (Square30x30 through Square310x310, StoreLogo), Linux PNG, and optionally iOS/Android icons (dirs exist as placeholders). macOS icons should use the liquid-glass style for iOS 26 / macOS Tahoe. Windows/Chrome icons should be clean and legible at small sizes.

subtasks.title: Design source icon (SVG or 1024px PNG)
Generate macOS .icns with all required sizes
Generate Windows .ico and Store PNGs
Generate Linux PNG variants
Create liquid-glass variant for macOS Tahoe / iOS 26

tasks.title: Code signing, notarization, and release pipeline
tasks.description: No release workflow exists for secrt-app yet. Need a GitHub Actions workflow (release-app.yml) triggered by app/vX.Y.Z tags that builds for macOS (universal binary), Windows (x64/arm64), and Linux (AppImage/deb). Must include macOS Developer ID signing + notarization and Windows Trusted Signing, mirroring the existing CLI release workflow. Without signing, users get 'unidentified developer' warnings and won't install.

subtasks.title: Create release-app.yml workflow skeleton
Configure macOS code signing and notarization
Configure Windows Trusted Signing
Build universal macOS binary (.dmg)
Build Windows installer (x64/arm64)
Build Linux packages (AppImage, deb)
Generate SHA-256 checksums and publish GitHub Release

tasks.title: Auto-clear clipboard after copying secrets
tasks.description:
Secrets copied to clipboard should be automatically cleared after a configurable timeout (default 30-60 seconds). Use Tauri's clipboard-manager plugin for native clipboard access. Add a visible countdown or toast so users know when the clipboard will be cleared. Should apply to: share link copies, claimed secret text copies, generated password copies. DEFERRED: UX frustration concerns outweigh marginal security benefit — if an attacker has clipboard access they likely have screen access too. Revisit if users request it.

Tracking: GitHub issue https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/issues/27 (open). Was deferred internally; consider re-activating to align with the public roadmap.

subtasks.title: Add tauri-plugin-clipboard-manager dependency
Update capabilities/default.json with clipboard permissions
Implement timed clipboard clear in clipboard.ts for Tauri
Add countdown toast/indicator for clipboard clear

tasks.title: Configure auto-updater
tasks.description: Add Tauri's updater plugin so the app can check for and install updates. Critical for a security tool — need to push fixes fast. Requires a signed update manifest hosted somewhere (GitHub Releases or a dedicated endpoint). The updater should check on app launch and optionally on a timer, with a non-intrusive notification when updates are available.

subtasks.title: Add tauri-plugin-updater dependency
Configure update endpoint (GitHub Releases or custom)
Implement update check on app launch
Add update notification UI
Sign update manifests in release pipeline

tasks.title: Register deep links for secrt.ca URLs
tasks.description:
Register the app to handle secrt.ca URLs and/or a secrt:// custom protocol so clicking a share link opens the desktop app instead of the browser. Tauri 2 has a deep-link plugin. macOS Universal Links require an apple-app-site-association file on the server. Windows uses URI protocol handlers. Linux uses .desktop file handlers. Consider also registering for secrt:// as a fallback protocol.

Tracking: GitHub issue https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/issues/30.

subtasks.title: Add tauri-plugin-deep-link dependency and permissions
Register secrt:// custom protocol on all platforms
Investigate macOS Universal Links (apple-app-site-association)
Handle incoming deep links and route to claim page
Test deep links on macOS, Windows, and Linux

tasks.title: Add keyboard shortcuts
tasks.description: Desktop app users expect standard keyboard shortcuts. Implement: Cmd/Ctrl+N (new secret), Cmd/Ctrl+V (paste link when no input focused), Cmd/Ctrl+, (settings), Cmd/Ctrl+W (close window). Use Tauri's global shortcut plugin or frontend key event handlers.

subtasks.title: Define shortcut map for all platforms
Implement shortcut handler (Tauri plugin or JS keydown)
Add Cmd+V global paste-link handler
Test shortcuts on macOS and Windows

tasks.title: Add About screen with version info
tasks.description: Users need to see what version they're running, especially for a security tool. Add an About screen or section (accessible from menu or settings) showing app version, build info, and links to the project. Tauri provides app version via the API.

subtasks.title: Create About page or modal with version, build, and links
Read version from Tauri app API
Add navigation to About from settings or menu

tasks.title: Harden CSP and audit permissions
tasks.description: Current CSP allows 'unsafe-inline' for scripts and styles. For production, tighten the CSP (nonces or hashes for inline scripts if possible). Audit capabilities/default.json to ensure only necessary permissions are granted — add clipboard, deep-link, updater permissions as needed but nothing extra. Review connect-src to ensure only secrt.ca is allowed.

subtasks.title: Audit and tighten CSP in tauri.conf.json
Audit capabilities/default.json for minimal permissions
Test that app functions correctly with tightened policies

tasks.title: Add file drag-and-drop support
tasks.description: The web app supports file upload via file picker, but native drag-and-drop onto the app window would improve UX. Tauri supports drag-drop events. When a file is dropped, populate the send form with the file contents. Optionally support dropping onto the dock icon (macOS) to launch with a file ready to send.

subtasks.title: Enable drag-drop in Tauri window config
Handle drop events and populate send form
Test with various file types and sizes

tasks.title: Add system tray with quick actions
tasks.description: Optional system tray icon for quick access. Could show: 'Create Secret' (opens main window), 'Paste Link' (claim from clipboard), 'Quit'. Useful for users who want the app always accessible without keeping a window open.

subtasks.title: Add tauri-plugin-tray dependency and icon
Configure tray menu items
Handle tray actions (open window, paste-claim, quit)

tasks.title: Add keychain storage for session and default passphrase
tasks.description:
Store session tokens and optional default passphrases in the OS keychain (macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, Linux Secret Service) instead of localStorage. The CLI already has keychain support. Use Tauri's stronghold plugin or a keychain plugin. Also support saving default encryption/decryption passphrases like the CLI config does. NOTE: The app already has keyring_set/keyring_get/keyring_delete Tauri commands with a file-based fallback for broken macOS Tahoe keychain. This task is about expanding usage to cover session tokens and passphrase storage, plus building the preferences UI (see task #37).

Tracking: GitHub issue https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/issues/28 (this task covers the keychain-storage half; the auto-decrypt UX half lives in task #38).

subtasks.title: Evaluate Tauri keychain/stronghold plugins
subtasks.description: Already using keyring crate with file-based fallback. Keyring commands (keyring_set/get/delete) already exist in the Tauri backend.

subtasks.title: Migrate session token storage from localStorage to keychain
Store default encryption passphrase in keychain
subtasks.description: Save a default passphrase (like CLI config 'passphrase') that is auto-applied when sending secrets. Stored via keyring_set with a dedicated key prefix.

subtasks.title: Store decryption passphrases list in keychain
subtasks.description: Save a list of passphrases to try automatically when claiming secrets (like CLI config 'decryption_passphrases'). Stored as JSON array via keyring.

tasks.title: Customize window chrome for native feel
tasks.description: The app currently looks like a webpage in a light wrapper. Make it feel like a real native app, starting with macOS. Use Tauri's window configuration for transparent/hidden titlebar, position traffic light buttons within the content area, add a custom drag region at the top of the layout. On Windows, consider custom titlebar or standard chrome (less jarring). May need vibrancy/blur effects on macOS for a polished look. The Nav component and Layout will need adjustments to account for the titlebar area.

subtasks.title: Configure transparent/hidden titlebar on macOS in tauri.conf.json
subtasks.description: Set titleBarStyle to 'overlay' or 'transparent' and hiddenTitle to true. Test with Tauri 2's macOS window options.

subtasks.title: Position traffic light buttons and add drag region
subtasks.description: Use Tauri's trafficLightPosition config. Add data-tauri-drag-region to a top bar element in Layout. Ensure the Nav component has enough top padding to not overlap traffic lights.

subtasks.title: Update Layout/Nav CSS for titlebar-integrated design
subtasks.description: Add platform-aware top padding. On macOS with hidden titlebar, the content needs ~28-32px top inset. Use Tauri's platform detection or CSS env(titlebar-area-*).

subtasks.title: Test on Windows and Linux
subtasks.description: Ensure standard window chrome works well on Windows/Linux. May keep default decorations there or add a custom titlebar later.

tasks.title: Track macOS keychain reliability across versions
tasks.description: macOS Tahoe 26 had a regression early in its lifecycle where SecItemAdd succeeded but SecItemCopyMatching returned errSecItemNotFound (-25300) for the same item in the same process, breaking the keyring crate (v3.6.3) entirely. We added a probe at startup and a file-based fallback at ~/Library/Application Support/ca.secrt.app/credentials.json that auto-activates when the probe fails. Direct same-process Swift probe on macOS 26.4.1 (build 25E253) on 2026-04-25 confirms the round-trip now works (SecItemAdd OSStatus=0 followed by SecItemCopyMatching OSStatus=0 returning the stored value). Apple has silently fixed the regression at some point between the original report and 26.4.1; their user-facing release notes don't itemize developer-API fixes so it isn't possible to pinpoint the exact point release. The file fallback stays in the codebase as defense-in-depth — the probe self-deactivates the fallback when the keychain works, so it costs ~80 lines and protects against future regressions, sandbox edge cases, or differing user environments. This task transitions from one-off fix-tracking to recurring stewardship: re-probe on each macOS major and on keyring crate upgrades.

subtasks.title: Verify keychain round-trip on macOS 26.4.1
subtasks.description: Same-process SecItemAdd → SecItemCopyMatching round-trip via Swift probe on jdlien's dev box (Tahoe 26.4.1, build 25E253). Both calls returned OSStatus 0; the stored value was retrieved correctly. Tested 2026-04-25.

subtasks.title: Re-probe on each new macOS major (e.g. 26.5, 27.x)
subtasks.description: Run the Swift probe (or `cargo test -p secrt-app keyring_probe_detects_broken_keychain -- --nocapture`) on a representative dev machine when each major macOS update lands. The point is not to wait for the bug to come back — it's to catch a recurrence before users notice. Any sign of regression: re-pin the file fallback as the default for the affected version range and surface a banner in the Tauri app.

subtasks.title: Try keyring crate v4.0.0-rc.3 against secrt-app
subtasks.description: v4 has been in RC since Feb 2025 with the v4 branch quiet since July 2025; no v4 stable yet. Try `keyring = "4.0.0-rc.3"` on a branch and see whether the API surface and apple-native backend still work for our use case. Low-stakes experiment — if v4 stable lands, this gets us ahead of the migration; if v4 stays in limbo, we lose nothing by knowing what changed. Run the existing keyring probe + the existing fallback round-trip test.

subtasks.title: Verify keychain behavior under signed/notarized production build
subtasks.description: The dev probe is unsigned. Production Tauri builds are code-signed with the Developer ID cert and have an Entitlements.plist applied. Subtle keychain behavior can differ between unsigned dev builds and signed release builds — sandbox restrictions, application groups, keychain access groups, etc. Once the release-app.yml workflow exists (task #21) and produces a signed .app, run the same Swift probe (or a Tauri command that exercises the keyring round-trip) inside the signed bundle on a clean Mac. If the signed-build behavior differs from the dev-build behavior, document and adjust.

subtasks.title: Decide: keep or remove the file-based fallback
subtasks.description: Decision: KEEP. The fallback is defense-in-depth. The probe at startup self-deactivates it when the keychain works, so on healthy systems it is a no-op. The cost (~80 lines in crates/secrt-app/src/lib.rs) is small. Removing it would trade a real safety margin (regressions, sandbox edge cases, user environments we have not seen) for a marginal code-cleanliness win. Re-evaluate if (a) keyring crate v4 stable ships with stronger guarantees, or (b) the fallback path is found to leak credentials to disk in a way the keychain wouldn't (which it does — the file is mode 600 in the user's app data dir, but is plaintext-on-disk; this is documented). Closing this subtask as the decision is made.

tasks.title: Add app preferences to Settings page
tasks.description:
Add a local Preferences section to the existing /settings page rather than creating a separate page. The /settings page currently requires auth (AuthGuard) — remove the auth gate so all users can access it, but conditionally show sections: Preferences cards (default TTL, auto-copy, theme) are visible to everyone; account cards (API keys, passkeys, display name, account deletion) remain visible only to authenticated users. This keeps navigation simple (one settings page) while letting unauthenticated users configure local preferences.

Preferences are local-only (per-device, not synced to server). Store in localStorage for web; use Tauri store plugin for desktop if available, falling back to localStorage. This task does NOT include passphrase management (that's task #38) — it builds the storage layer and UI foundation that #38 extends.

The existing Nav already links to /settings for authenticated users. For unauthenticated users, add a gear icon or similar entry point so they can reach preferences without logging in.

subtasks.title: Create preferences store module
subtasks.description: Build web/src/lib/preferences.ts with get/set/subscribe. Use localStorage (works for both web and Tauri). Keys: default_ttl (string, e.g. '24h'), auto_copy (boolean, default true), theme (string: 'system'|'light'|'dark'). Provide a usePreference(key) hook that returns [value, setValue] and re-renders on change.

subtasks.title: Remove AuthGuard from Settings page, add conditional sections
subtasks.description: Remove AuthGuard wrapping from the Settings route. In SettingsPage, render Preferences cards unconditionally at the top. Wrap existing account sections (API keys, passkeys, account management) in an auth check — show them only when logged in, or show a 'Log in to manage your account' prompt.

subtasks.title: Add Preferences card to Settings page
subtasks.description: New card(s) at the top of SettingsPage: General section with default TTL dropdown (matching the TTL options from SendPage's selector) and auto-copy share link toggle. Appearance section with theme selector (system/light/dark — may replace or complement the existing Nav toggle). Use the same card styling as existing settings sections.

subtasks.title: Wire default TTL into SendPage
subtasks.description: SendPage's TTL selector should initialize from the saved default_ttl preference instead of its hardcoded default. If no preference saved, keep current default behavior.

subtasks.title: Add Settings link to Nav for unauthenticated users
subtasks.description: Currently Nav only shows Settings link when logged in (via user menu). Add a gear icon or 'Settings' link visible to all users so they can reach preferences without an account. Logged-in users still see it in their user menu as before.

tasks.title: Add saved passphrases and auto-decryption
tasks.description:
Port the CLI's passphrase management to the app. Two features: (1) Default encryption passphrase — automatically applied when creating secrets (user can override or clear per-secret). (2) Decryption passphrases list — when claiming a secret, automatically try: no passphrase → default passphrase → each saved decryption passphrase → prompt user. This mirrors the CLI's multi-phase auto-decryption from config.toml. Passphrases are stored in the OS keychain via existing keyring_set/get commands (with file fallback on broken macOS). The Settings page (task #37) provides the management UI as a new Passphrases card in the Preferences section.

Note: saved passphrases are a desktop-only feature (requires Tauri keyring commands). On web, this section should either be hidden or show a message directing users to the desktop app.

Tracking: GitHub issue https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/issues/28 (this task covers the saved-passphrases UX half; the keychain-storage primitive lives in task #34).

subtasks.title: Add passphrase management card to Settings page
subtasks.description: New 'Passphrases' card in the Preferences section of Settings. Contains: (a) Default encryption passphrase — masked input with show/hide toggle, save/clear buttons. (b) Decryption passphrases — ordered list with add/remove/reorder. All stored via Tauri keyring commands. Desktop-only: hide or show 'desktop app required' on web.

subtasks.title: Auto-apply default passphrase on send
subtasks.description: When SendPage loads, check for a saved default passphrase. If present, pre-populate the passphrase field (or apply silently). Show indicator that default passphrase is active. Allow user to clear/override per-secret. Add a 'no passphrase' toggle to explicitly skip.

subtasks.title: Implement multi-phase auto-decryption on claim
subtasks.description: When claiming a secret that requires a passphrase, try in order: (1) no passphrase, (2) default passphrase, (3) each decryption passphrase from the saved list. If all fail, show the passphrase prompt as today. Show a brief status like 'Trying saved passphrases...' during auto-attempts. Don't leak which passphrase worked.

subtasks.title: Add passphrase import/export for CLI config parity
subtasks.description: Optional: allow importing passphrases from CLI config.toml or exporting to it, so users who use both CLI and app can share their passphrase lists.

tasks.title: Auto-copy share link after secret creation
tasks.description: After successfully creating a secret, automatically copy the share link to the clipboard using copySensitive (which excludes from clipboard history on all platforms). The CLI does this by default via auto_copy config. Add a preference toggle (auto_copy in task #37's preferences store) so users can disable it. Show a brief toast or visual indicator confirming the copy. Do NOT auto-copy claimed secret text — that stays as a manual CopyButton action.

subtasks.title: Auto-copy share link in ShareResult component
subtasks.description: After the share URL is generated and displayed, automatically call copySensitive(shareUrl). Respect the auto_copy preference. Show a toast or checkmark confirming 'Link copied to clipboard'.

subtasks.title: Add auto_copy toggle to Settings preferences
subtasks.description: Toggle in the Preferences section of Settings page under General. Default: on. When off, share link is displayed but not auto-copied (user clicks CopyButton manually as today).

tasks.title: Add Icelandic localization (is-IS)
tasks.description: Localize the SPA to Icelandic for the secrt.is deployment. Wire up i18n (currently English-only), externalize all UI strings, and translate. Out of scope: server-emitted strings (robots.txt comments, error responses) which are already English. Consider host-aware default locale (secrt.is → is-IS, secrt.ca → en-CA) with a manual override. Driven by the new secrt.is presence in Iceland.

tasks.title: Document jurisdiction choices (Iceland + Canada)
tasks.description: Document the operational deployments. **Iceland deployment shipped on 1984 Hosting (secrt.is)**, live since 2026-04-22 — full deployment recipe at /Users/jdlien/code/getsecrt/2026-04-22-21-45_secrt-is-iceland-deployment-guide.md (Ubuntu 24.04 / 1GB / Caddy + Rust binary + Postgres, hardened, Ubuntu Pro + Livepatch). **Canada deployment continues at secrt.ca**. Both deployments consume the same prebuilt Rust binary from GitHub Releases — single codebase, two jurisdictional surfaces. Iceland choice rationale: among the strongest privacy regimes in the world (IMMI legacy, strong data protection, free-expression precedent, lower MLAT-on-demand exposure); 1984 Hosting specifically chosen for ideological alignment and history of resisting law-enforcement pressure. Caveat: 1984 is still IPv4-only; IPv6 has been 'coming soon' for 13+ years and they confirmed by email it remains their current stance. Canada rationale: rule of law plus data residency for Canadian-regulated businesses (PIPEDA-aligned, hosted in-country). Frame as both a privacy story and a B2B residency story.

subtasks.title: Add jurisdiction section to How it Works
subtasks.description: Plain-language version on web/src/features/trust/HowItWorksPage.tsx — 3-5 sentences for casual users. Cover: two deployments because jurisdiction matters to the threat model; Iceland for strongest privacy regime; Canada for data residency + working with Canadian-regulated businesses. Link to the whitepaper for depth.

subtasks.title: Add jurisdiction section to whitepaper
subtasks.description: Citations-backed treatment in docs/whitepaper.md. Lift operational details from the secrt.is deployment guide. Specific statutes (Icelandic Act on the Protection of Privacy 90/2018, PIPEDA, Charter), MLAT exposure analysis, why Iceland and Canada were chosen over US/UK/EU, 1984 Hosting's track record. Note the IPv4-only limitation honestly. Aimed at security researchers and B2B procurement reviewers.

tasks.title: Passkey PRF-based AMK wrapping (zero-knowledge passkey UX)
tasks.description:
**Design doc:** `crates/secrt-server/docs/prf-amk-wrapping.md` — read this first. Contains the exact KDF formulas, AAD layout, wire format, schema migration, HTTP route definitions, flow diagrams, threat model, test-vector template, and open questions. The summary below is a map; the doc is the territory.

Add WebAuthn PRF extension as an alternative path for deriving the AMK wrap-key, so new devices can recover the AMK by signing in with a passkey instead of scanning a sync-link QR. This is the single biggest UX win available for the zero-knowledge notes product — it replicates the 'walk up, tap passkey, see your stuff' flow that Proton/Standard Notes can't offer because they still require a master password.

## What stays the same
The AMK itself (random 32-byte key), AES-256-GCM envelope encryption, note encryption (HKDF(AMK, salt, 'secrt-note-v1')), the amk_wrappers table structure, the commitment hash, the versioning, the AAD binding, envelope encryption, url_key, Argon2id passphrases. This is purely an additive path at the 'how do we derive wrap_key' layer.

## What changes
Current: wrap_key = HKDF(api_key_root, root_salt, 'secrt-amk-wrap-v1', 32), keyed per-API-key.
New: wrap_key = HKDF(prf_output, cred_salt, 'secrt-amk-wrap-prf-v1', 32), keyed per-passkey-credential.
Server adds a new amk_wrappers row variant keyed by credential_id instead of api_key_prefix. Both paths coexist.

## Browser support matrix (as of April 2026)
- Chrome/Edge on Windows 11 25H2 + Windows Hello: Chrome 147+ (PRF-on-create landed Feb 2026)
- Safari 18+ on iOS 18.4+ / macOS 15+ with iCloud Keychain: works
- Chrome on Android + Google Password Manager: PRF-by-default, most mature
- Firefox 148+: first Firefox with full PRF support (user share still small)
- External security keys on iOS Safari: broken (hmac-secret returned encrypted) — fall back to sync-link

PRF is an ENHANCEMENT, not a replacement. Keep the existing ECDH/QR sync-link flow for: Firefox ≤147, iOS Safari + external key, account recovery, users who prefer the explicit key-in-hand mental model.

## Key technical gotcha
The PRF salt must be STABLE per credential (domain separator, not a nonce). Generate at registration with crypto.getRandomValues(), persist server-side alongside the credential record. Reusing the same salt across devices that sync the same passkey (iCloud Keychain, Google PM) is correct and required for PRF-output determinism across the synced set.

## Affected code
- web/src/crypto/amk.ts — new deriveAmkWrapKeyFromPrf() alongside existing deriveAmkWrapKey()
- web/src/features/passkeys/ — registration requests PRF extension; login calls PRF at auth time
- secrt-core/src/amk.rs — add prf-variant wrap-key derivation path
- secrt-server migrations — extend amk_wrappers with credential_id column + index
- secrt-server passkeys routes — store cred_salt at registration; expose wrapped AMK by credential_id
- spec/v1/server.md — document PRF app-login variant
- spec/v1/envelope.md — no changes expected (orthogonal concern)

subtasks.title: Research spike: prototype PRF-on-create + PRF-on-get end-to-end
subtasks.description: Standalone HTML/TS prototype in web/ (outside the app shell) that: registers a passkey with PRF extension requested (evaluate: { first: <32-byte random salt> }), stores the salt in localStorage + a mock server blob, then authenticates and derives the same PRF output. Verify on all four target surfaces: Chrome+Windows Hello, Safari+iCloud Keychain (macOS and iOS), Chrome+Android+Google PM. Confirm synced passkey PRF determinism across devices (create on macOS Safari, use on iOS Safari). Document any browser bugs or caveats discovered; update the browser support matrix in the main task description if reality differs.

subtasks.title: Server: extend amk_wrappers + passkey credentials for PRF path
subtasks.description: See `crates/secrt-server/docs/prf-amk-wrapping.md` §3.3 (schema) + §3.5 (HTTP routes) + §8 (implementation plan). Migration: add cred_salt (BYTEA, 32 bytes) and prf_supported (BOOL) to webauthn_credentials (or equivalent table). Extend amk_wrappers to be keyable by either api_key_prefix (existing) or credential_id (new) — likely a discriminator column and a nullable fk pair. Add server routes: PUT /api/v1/auth/passkeys/{cred_id}/amk-wrapper (store a PRF-wrapped AMK after user completes registration and client has computed it), GET /api/v1/auth/passkeys/{cred_id}/amk-wrapper (return the wrapped blob at login time, rate-limited, requires fresh session). Index for lookup by credential_id. Update spec/v1/server.md with new endpoints.

subtasks.title: Client: passkey registration requests PRF extension + persists salt
subtasks.description: In the registration ceremony (features/passkeys/register), add `extensions: { prf: { eval: { first: new Uint8Array(salt32) } } }`. Generate salt32 with crypto.getRandomValues(). POST salt32 to server alongside the credential creation response — stored on webauthn_credentials.cred_salt. Inspect clientExtensionResults.prf to detect PRF support: if prf.enabled is true, mark credential as PRF-capable server-side; if false or undefined, credential is legacy and the user will use sync-link for this credential only. Do NOT fail registration if PRF unsupported — fall through gracefully.

subtasks.title: Client: PRF wrap/unwrap crypto path
subtasks.description: See `crates/secrt-server/docs/prf-amk-wrapping.md` §3.1 (derivation formula), §3.2 (AAD layout), §9 (test vectors — fill in before landing). In web/src/crypto/amk.ts and secrt-core/src/amk.rs, add deriveAmkWrapKeyFromPrf(prfOutput: Uint8Array, credSalt: Uint8Array) -> wrap_key, using HKDF-SHA256 with info='secrt-amk-wrap-prf-v1'. Reuse existing WrappedAmk { ct, nonce, version } structure and AAD format (bind to credential_id instead of api_key_prefix for PRF wrappers). Add unit tests round-tripping wrap→unwrap with known test vectors. Ensure AAD binding prevents cross-credential replay.

subtasks.title: Client: login flow fetches + unwraps PRF-wrapped AMK
subtasks.description: See `crates/secrt-server/docs/prf-amk-wrapping.md` §4.4 (new-device login flow diagram) + §4.5 (upgrade path). After successful passkey authentication that returned a PRF output: fetch GET /api/v1/auth/passkeys/{cred_id}/amk-wrapper, unwrap with the PRF-derived wrap_key, store AMK in IndexedDB via existing storeAmk(). If the credential has no PRF wrapper yet (first login with a new PRF-capable credential), trigger a one-time 'upgrade' flow: derive AMK via the existing path (user may need to sync-link from another device), then create a PRF wrapper for this credential so future logins skip the sync-link step. Handle the 'PRF not supported on this browser today' case by falling through to sync-link.

subtasks.title: Feature detection + fallback UX
subtasks.description: Detect PRF support at page load (PublicKeyCredential.isConditionalMediationAvailable + test PRF extension echo on a dummy assertion). Settings page shows per-credential status: 'Passkey login derives your key automatically' (PRF-enabled) vs 'This credential uses sync-link key transfer' (legacy). Registration flow copy: when PRF supported, 'Your passkey will unlock your notes automatically on new devices.' When PRF not supported: 'You'll use the sync-link flow on new devices until your browser adds passkey-key-derivation support.' Recovery messaging: make it explicit that losing all passkeys means data loss unless user has a sync-link recovery code somewhere.

subtasks.title: Tests + browser matrix CI
subtasks.description:
Unit tests for wrap/unwrap round-trip in both Rust and TS. Integration test for register-with-PRF → store wrapper → login on different 'device' (different IndexedDB) → unwrap → verify AMK bytes match. Playwright browser matrix job covering Chrome desktop, Safari desktop, Chrome Android (if feasible). Manual test script for iCloud Keychain sync across macOS+iOS. Document the CI browser matrix in CONTRIBUTING.md.

Explicit scenario — CLI bootstrap with PRF: end-to-end test of `secrt auth login` in a brand-new browser profile (no prior AMK in IndexedDB). Expected: verification-URL page triggers PRF login, unwraps server-stored AMK, user taps Approve, ECDH handoff to CLI succeeds, CLI wraps under api-key-root and stores via `/amk-wrappers`. Today this scenario requires the user to paste an API key or run `sync` first; with PRF it should be single-tap. Covered in design doc §4.6.

subtasks.title: Blog post: 'How we replaced the master password with a passkey without giving up zero-knowledge'
subtasks.description: Technical deep-dive blog post on jdlien's blog (webartisan.info) explaining: the zero-knowledge UX problem, why existing services still use master passwords, how WebAuthn PRF works, the salt design, the fallback strategy, what the final UX looks like. Target audience: privacy-conscious developers who will then try secrt and tell friends. Dovetails with the April 'blog weekly' commitment (FOCUS.md Big Rocks). Draft after Phase 4-5 land so screenshots are real.

tasks.title: Explore warrant canary for secrt
tasks.description:
Decide whether to ship a warrant canary, and if so, of what kind. The architecture is secrt's primary privacy defense; a canary is auxiliary signal about whether that architecture has been compromised under legal compulsion. Key insight from design conversation (2026-04-24): for a zero-knowledge service whose main threat vector is compelled code modification (NSL/CSIS/CSE order to ship a backdoored client), the most potent canary is release-signed rather than page-published. A README canary tells users 'the server is honest'; a release-signed canary tells users 'this specific binary has not been tampered with under legal compulsion.' That binds the canary to the actual attack surface. Canadian legal ground (Charter s.2(b) vs. s.1 reasonable-limits) is less tested than US First Amendment doctrine — Iceland is friendlier ground. Tie wording to the jurisdiction story established in task 41. Don't auto-update from a cron — signing must require manual action with a key under exclusive control, otherwise the signal is meaningless. Pain to operate, but architecturally honest.

[Updated 2026-04-24] Iceland deployment is now live at secrt.is on 1984 Hosting. Both deployments consume the same signed Rust binary from GitHub Releases, which means **one release-signed canary attests binary integrity across both jurisdictional surfaces** — that's the architectural simplification. Per-instance page canaries (one for secrt.ca, one for secrt.is) remain useful for jurisdiction-specific events (preservation demands, gag orders specific to the host country, 1984 Hosting being compelled by Icelandic authorities). The canary architecture maps cleanly: shared release attestation + per-jurisdiction page canaries.

subtasks.title: Decide canary form: release-signed vs. published-page vs. both
subtasks.description: Evaluate three options. (a) Release-signed: every signed release announcement contains a dated canary clause covering compelled code/key/config changes — binds to the compromise vector but only signals on release cadence. (b) Published-page: signed statement on a /canary URL updated every 14 days — independent of release cadence, more visible, but doesn't bind to binary integrity. (c) Both, with the page canary as the user-facing visible thing and the release canary as the integrity assertion. Recommendation going in: (c), but verify by writing out what each one actually claims and what an adversary would have to compel to defeat it.

subtasks.title: Draft canary statement wording (CA + IS instruments)
subtasks.description: Specific over vague — vague canaries invite the order-issuer to argue 'we didn't compel exactly what the canary denied.' Wording should explicitly cover: National Security Letters / FISA-equivalent orders; CSIS Act s.21 warrants; CSE Act preservation demands; Canadian anti-terrorism framework production orders; Icelandic instruments (Act on Criminal Procedure 88/2008 production orders, telecommunications interception under the Police Act — research the actual list since the surface is much narrower than CA/US); 1984 Hosting being compelled to act against secrt.is specifically; compelled code modification to the shared Rust binary; compelled cryptographic key disclosure; compelled retention beyond stated policy; gag orders preventing canary update. Have a Canadian privacy lawyer review if budget allows; consider an Icelandic counterpart for the IS-specific wording. Model on rsync.net's wording with our jurisdiction-specific additions.

subtasks.title: Operational design — key custody, cadence, lapse policy, bus factor
subtasks.description: Signing key for the canary MUST be under exclusive personal control — not in CI, not in the deployment pipeline, not auto-callable. Cold storage or hardware token. Cadence: every 14 days for the page canary; per-release for the release canary. Lapse policy: published doc says 'a lapse of more than 21 days should be treated as a real signal.' Bus-factor handover: documented procedure for what happens if JD is unreachable (does the canary lapse intentionally? does a co-trustee with a separate key take over? this is the hardest operational question and needs a written answer before launch).

subtasks.title: Legal posture writeup for whitepaper
subtasks.description: Document the legal theory under which canaries are believed to work in each jurisdiction we operate in. Canada: Charter s.2(b) free expression protection vs. s.1 reasonable-limits override; theory is that compelled silence is constitutionally distinct from compelled speech, but not yet tested in court for canaries specifically. Iceland: much more permissive privacy regime, fewer national-security gag instruments to worry about, IMMI legacy. US users (whose data goes to either deployment): note that the canary doesn't protect them from US legal process directed at the host country, only signals compromise of the host. Be honest in the whitepaper that Canadian canary law is unsettled — this is partly an act of legal-theory-making, not just legal-theory-following.

subtasks.title: Ship v1: page canary + release-canary clause + how-to-read-this doc
subtasks.description: Public /canary URL with the signed statement. Release announcement template updated to include the canary clause. Trust-page link to /canary alongside the existing How it Works. 'How to read this canary' explainer for users — covers what it claims, what it doesn't, what a lapse means, where to verify the signature. Link from task 41's jurisdiction docs.

subtasks.title: Optional: blog post on canary design choices
subtasks.description: 'Why secrt has a canary, and why most services' canaries are doing the wrong job.' Argument: most canaries protect against the wrong threat (server-side data compulsion) for services whose actual exposure is client-side code compulsion. Position secrt's release-signed canary as the more honest design. Aligns with the broader public-argument work (FOCUS.md Big Rocks → blog cadence). Not blocking for v1 ship; queue when blog rhythm is reestablished.

tasks.title: Polish getsecrt GitHub org profile page
tasks.description:
Current state of github.com/getsecrt is bare: org description 'A zero-knowledge secret sharing service' is fine, blog points to secrt.app, location says Canada (out of date once Iceland deployment ships per task 41), only public repos are 'secrt' (Rust monorepo, 2 stars) and 'spec' (protocol spec, no description on the repo level). No org profile README, so the landing page is just a member/repo list. Visitors arriving from a blog post, HN, or word-of-mouth land on something that looks abandoned. A polished org profile is part of the public-argument layer — a serious privacy project has to *look* serious, especially when the architectural posture is the differentiator. This is the GitHub-side counterpart to the secrt.app marketing site. Connects to the April 'Build' Big Rock (FOCUS.md) — making the work visible is half the commitment.

[Updated 2026-04-24] Iceland deployment now live at secrt.is — location field can read 'Distributed (CA + IS)' for real, no more 'once Iceland ships' hedging. secrt.app currently 301-redirects to secrt.ca and is unallocated as a marketing surface. Open product question (see new subtask 7): should secrt.app become a dedicated landing page with download buttons + concise pitch, or redirect to the polished GitHub org profile once shipped? JD's leaning download-buttons + pitch — that's the more user-facing answer if app distribution is part of the product story.

subtasks.title: Create .github repo with profile/README.md
subtasks.description: GitHub convention: a repo literally named '.github' in the org, containing 'profile/README.md', renders as the org landing page. Create the repo public, add the README, verify it renders on github.com/getsecrt before iterating on content.

subtasks.title: Write the profile README hero content
subtasks.description: The README is the landing pitch. Structure roughly: (1) one-sentence what-it-is — 'Zero-knowledge one-time secret sharing. The server can't read your secrets — by design, not by promise.' (2) The threat model in three bullets — sender-side encryption, ephemeral links, no server-side plaintext access. (3) Jurisdiction story (lift from task 41 once that lands) — Iceland for strongest privacy regime, Canada for residency-required B2B. (4) Links: secrt.app live service, secrt/spec repos, whitepaper, /canary (when task 43 ships). (5) A 'why this exists' paragraph — short version of the privacy-as-moral-claim argument from the Apr 24 conversation. Don't bury the lede in marketing copy; the audience is technical and skeptical.

subtasks.title: Add visual assets — logo, architecture diagram, maybe a screenshot
subtasks.description: GitHub READMEs benefit from one strong above-the-fold visual. Options: (a) the secrt wordmark/logo if there is one, (b) a small architecture diagram showing client-side encryption / server-stores-blob / ephemeral-link flow, (c) a screenshot of the live UI. Probably (b) — most legible to the technical audience. Keep it SVG so it scales and stays crisp on dark/light mode. Host the asset in the .github repo itself so it doesn't break if other repos move.

subtasks.title: Sharpen org-level metadata
subtasks.description: Update org settings: description (current is fine but could be sharper — 'Zero-knowledge one-time secret sharing — the server can't read your secrets by design'); **location should read 'Distributed (CA + IS)' now that secrt.is is live**; blog URL — verify which is the intended canonical (secrt.app, secrt.ca, or secrt.is); add social links if any (Mastodon? Bluesky? Twitter is dead for this audience). Pinned repos: pin 'secrt' and 'spec' so they appear at the top above the .github repo.

subtasks.title: Audit existing repos' READMEs as click-through destinations
subtasks.description: The org page sends visitors to 'secrt' and 'spec'. Both READMEs need to survive the click — same standard as the org page. 'secrt' is a Rust monorepo and probably has a working README, but verify. 'spec' currently has no repo-level description and likely a thin README — the protocol spec deserves a proper landing (one-paragraph what-the-spec-is, table of contents, link to test vectors). Don't let the polish of the org page get undermined by a stub README on the click-through.

subtasks.title: Clean up demo-repository
subtasks.description: Private repo 'demo-repository' with description 'A code repository designed to show the best GitHub has to offer' — almost certainly GitHub-auto-generated boilerplate from when the org was created. Confirm it's not in use, then delete (or archive if there's any reason to keep it). Cosmetic but the public_repos count and any future audit should reflect intent.

subtasks.title: Decide secrt.app's purpose (landing page vs. redirect to org page)
subtasks.description: secrt.app currently 301-redirects to secrt.ca — wasted asset. Two options: (a) Build a marketing landing page with download buttons for the desktop apps, a concise pitch ('zero-knowledge one-time secret sharing — the server can't read your secrets by design'), and links to both jurisdictional instances. (b) Redirect to the polished GitHub org page once it ships. (a) is more user-facing and matches a product that distributes apps; (b) is lower-effort if the org page is doing the work. JD's lean: (a). Decide before doing the work for either, and document the decision so it doesn't have to be re-litigated.

tasks.title: Design jurisdiction-picker UX (which instance am I using, how do I choose)
tasks.description: Problem identified by JD on 2026-04-24: with Iceland (secrt.is) and Canada (secrt.ca) both live, users currently land on whichever domain they hit, with no obvious indication that they are operating under different legal jurisdictions with materially different privacy properties. The .is deployment shows the IS TLD in user-facing URLs and uses security@secrt.is, but there's no explicit 'you are using the Iceland instance' surface. The whole point of running a second jurisdiction is to give users a real choice — and right now the choice is invisible. This task is the UX that makes the architectural value legible. Touches the web app, the desktop apps (Tauri), and the marketing surface (secrt.app).

subtasks.title: Define the user model — when does jurisdiction matter, to whom
subtasks.description: Three rough user segments: (1) Casual consumer sharing a quick secret — probably doesn't care, defaults are fine, but should be able to see which instance they're on. (2) Privacy-motivated user — wants explicit control, will pick deliberately. (3) B2B / Canadian-regulated business — *needs* secrt.ca for residency reasons, must not accidentally land on .is. Decide what the default routing is for each, what the explicit choice surface looks like, and whether sign-in remembers the choice. Document before designing UI.

subtasks.title: Prototype the picker UI — settings menu vs. first-visit prompt vs. footer indicator
subtasks.description: Three plausible surfaces: (a) Settings menu — hidden until invoked, good for privacy-motivated users who go looking. (b) First-visit prompt — banner or modal asking 'Iceland or Canada?' with a one-line explanation of why it matters. Explicit but potentially friction-y. (c) Persistent footer indicator — 'You are using secrt.is (Iceland)' with a switch link. Most legible, lowest-friction, doesn't interrupt. Recommendation going in: (c) as the always-visible signal + (a) for explicit control. Avoid (b) for casual users; consider it for B2B account creation.

subtasks.title: Implement instance switching across web + Tauri apps
subtasks.description: Web is straightforward — link/redirect to the other domain. Desktop apps are harder: the Tauri client needs to know which API endpoint to talk to, and the choice needs to persist. Decide whether the Tauri app picks instance at first launch and stores it, or supports per-secret instance selection. The shared Rust core code presumably already abstracts the endpoint — verify and extend if needed.

subtasks.title: Plain-language jurisdiction explainer for the picker
subtasks.description: When a user clicks 'why does this matter,' they should land on a 2-3 paragraph explainer: jurisdictions differ in what legal demands they accept, Iceland has stronger default privacy protections (cite IMMI / 1984 Hosting), Canada offers PIPEDA residency for businesses that need it. Don't overstate the user-visible difference — both deployments share the zero-knowledge architecture, so the *encrypted* content is equivalently safe. The difference is in metadata, host-country legal exposure, and compliance fit. Be honest about what each instance *does* and *doesn't* protect against.

subtasks.title: Iron out URL/email inconsistencies on secrt.is
subtasks.description: JD flagged on 2026-04-24 that 'a couple exceptions' show the wrong TLD or wrong support email on the .is deployment. Audit the codebase for hardcoded secrt.ca references, hardcoded support@ / security@ addresses, hardcoded URLs in emails or in-app strings. Either drive everything from a single per-instance config struct or runtime env, or grep the codebase exhaustively. Lower-priority cleanup but blocks the credibility of the instance-switcher work — if the .is instance still says secrt.ca in three places, the jurisdiction story leaks.

tasks.title: In-CLI update check and self-update against GitHub Releases
tasks.description:
Right now an old secrt CLI talking to a new server fails in cryptic ways (e.g., the v0.15.0 AAD format break: a 0.14.x CLI uploads a wrapper the new server stores but the new client cannot unwrap). For a security-sensitive tool this is two problems at once: (1) users may be running a version with a known vulnerability without noticing, and (2) the server cannot evolve its protocol without leaving stale clients in a silently-broken state. Solve both by having the CLI check GitHub Releases on a cached cadence, surface a clear stderr banner when out of date, and provide a single-command upgrade path that re-verifies the SHA-256 checksum the release workflow already publishes. GitHub Releases are signed (Apple Developer ID + Azure Trusted Signing for the macOS/Windows artifacts) and public, so no new infrastructure is required and supply-chain integrity is preserved end-to-end. Scope: this task is the CLI update story only — Homebrew, apt/RPM, and other package managers are deliberately out of scope until there is concrete user demand. The optional installer subtask covers first-time setup; everything after that goes through `secrt update`.

Tracking: GitHub issue https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/issues/2 (project: https://github.com/orgs/getsecrt/projects/1).

subtasks.title: Spec: normative update-check + self-update behavior in spec/v1/cli.md
subtasks.description: Before code: write a new section in spec/v1/cli.md (likely under HTTP Client Behavior) that defines the contract. Decisions to settle now, in writing: (a) Where does the latest version come from — direct GitHub API (api.github.com/repos/getsecrt/secrt/releases/latest filtered to cli/v* tags) or a server-provided field on /api/v1/info? Recommend GitHub direct because it is independent of any single secrt instance, cacheable via standard HTTP, and survives a server outage. (b) What is the cache cadence? 24h is a reasonable default, longer is wasteful, shorter risks GitHub rate-limit on heavy users. (c) Where is the cache stored? Recommend ~/.cache/secrt/update-check.json (XDG cache home), separate from the config dir so config-only deletion does not nuke the cache and vice versa. (d) When does the check fire? Once per command at most, only after the command's primary work completes (so a slow GitHub API call does not block secrt send), and never when stdin/stdout is being used for binary data. (e) What is the opt-out? --no-update-check flag and update_check = false config key, plus an environment variable SECRT_NO_UPDATE_CHECK=1 for CI/scripted contexts. (f) What output goes where? The banner is one dim line on STDERR — never stdout, never multi-line, never colorized when stderr is not a TTY. Suggested wording: secrt 0.16.0 is available (you have 0.15.0). Run 'secrt update' to upgrade. Audit before merge: must not break the existing 'pipe-friendly stdout' invariant the CLI spec already commits to.

subtasks.title: Implement the cached version check (read path)
subtasks.description: Add a small module (suggested: crates/secrt-cli/src/update_check.rs) that exposes one entry point: pub fn check(deps: &Deps) -> Option<UpdateAvailable>. Behavior: read ~/.cache/secrt/update-check.json (or the platform-equivalent cache dir). If the cached checked_at is within the configured interval (default 24h), use the cached latest_version. Otherwise issue a single GET to https://api.github.com/repos/getsecrt/secrt/releases?per_page=30 with a 5s timeout, filter for tags matching cli/v\d+\.\d+\.\d+ (skip drafts and prereleases), pick the highest semver, and write the result back to the cache file. On any error (timeout, parse error, IO error) return None and silently move on — an update check must never break a working CLI invocation. Use the existing ureq client to avoid adding a new HTTP dep. Return Some(UpdateAvailable { latest, current }) only if latest > current. Test coverage: inject a stub HTTP fn via the existing Deps pattern, table-test the cache hit/miss paths, verify that a 503 from GitHub is silent.

subtasks.title: Wire the banner into the command dispatcher
subtasks.description: After the primary command's output has been written and just before exit, call update_check::check() and emit the banner if Some(_) is returned. Wiring rules: (a) The banner is suppressed when --silent, --no-update-check, update_check = false in config, or SECRT_NO_UPDATE_CHECK=1 in env. (b) The banner is also suppressed when --json is set (machine-readable mode must stay clean). (c) The banner is suppressed when the current command is secrt update itself (avoid double-talking). (d) If stderr is not a TTY, drop ANSI color codes but still emit the line — pipelines that grep stderr for warnings should still work. Add an e2e-tier test that sets SECRT_NO_UPDATE_CHECK=1 and verifies stderr is exactly the expected output (no banner). Write a separate test that points the check fn at a fake 'newer version available' result and verifies the banner appears once and only once.

subtasks.title: Implement `secrt update` (self-update) subcommand
subtasks.description: Adds a top-level command that downloads the right release artifact for the current OS+arch, verifies the SHA-256 against secrt-checksums-sha256.txt from the same release, and replaces the running binary atomically. OS+arch detection: cargo-style (std::env::consts::OS / ARCH) -> asset name mapping (secrt-linux-amd64.tar.gz, secrt-linux-arm64.tar.gz, secrt-macos-arm64.pkg or the universal secrt-macos.pkg, secrt-windows-amd64.zip, etc.). On macOS, decide whether to handle the .pkg directly (preferred — keeps notarization intact) or fall back to extracting a tarball; check what artifacts the release-cli workflow currently publishes per platform. Atomic replace strategy: download to a temp file in the same directory as the running binary (so the rename does not cross filesystems), chmod 755, then rename(2) over the running file. On Unix this works because the kernel keeps the inode of the running process alive after rename. On Windows it does not — the binary file is locked while running — so the implementation needs to write to secrt.exe.new, schedule a MoveFileEx(MOVEFILE_DELAY_UNTIL_REBOOT) or use a self-restart shim. Document the Windows quirk in the help text: 'on Windows, the new version takes effect after the next launch.' Flags: --check (just print whether an update is available, no download), --force (download and reinstall even if already on latest), --version <X.Y.Z> (pin to a specific version, useful for downgrades during incident response). Always re-verify the SHA-256, even with --force. Never trust the existing local binary as a check against the new one — verify against the published checksum file every time. The gh CLI's own gh extension upgrade is a useful reference implementation.

subtasks.title: Server-side min_supported_cli_version field on /api/v1/info (optional but recommended)
subtasks.description: Add an optional min_supported_cli_version field to the /api/v1/info response. The CLI checks this on every server request that hits /api/v1/info (which secrt auth status and similar already do) and prints a hard 'this CLI is too old to talk to this server safely — please upgrade' message before continuing. This complements the GitHub-Releases-based check by giving the server an authoritative way to declare a hard floor — useful for situations like the v0.15.0 AAD break where some commands are silently broken on old clients. The field is advisory only: the server does not refuse old clients (that would break the secrt get zero-knowledge bearer flow for anyone who got a link before they upgraded). The CLI uses the field to inform the user, not to block them. Spec change in spec/v1/api.md and spec/v1/server.md; default behavior when the field is absent is to fall back to the GitHub-Releases check.

subtasks.title: User-friendly error reporting for failed self-updates
subtasks.description: When secrt update fails (network error, SHA-256 mismatch, write-protected install path, /usr/local/bin not writable without sudo, etc.), the error message must tell the user exactly what happened and what to try next: (a) network errors -> suggest --proxy env vars, retry, or manual download from the printed release URL; (b) SHA-256 mismatch -> halt immediately, do not install, surface both expected and actual hashes (this is a security-sensitive failure and must be loud); (c) permission-denied on the install path -> suggest re-running with sudo secrt update or specifying --install-dir <path>; (d) Windows file-lock -> fall back to the deferred-rename path automatically, tell the user the upgrade lands at next launch. Log nothing remotely — this is a privacy tool. The user is the only audience for these errors.

subtasks.title: First-time installer script at secrt.ca/install.sh (optional)
subtasks.description: Companion to the in-CLI updater for first-time users who do not have the binary yet. A single bash script that detects OS+arch, pulls the matching artifact from the latest GitHub release, verifies the published SHA-256, and installs to /usr/local/bin/secrt (or ~/bin/secrt if /usr/local is not writable). Usage pattern is the standard pipe-to-shell one popularized by Rustup, Bun, and similar tools. Mitigations for the well-known reputational issue: (a) source-mirror the script in the repo so it can be audited by URL: visit github.com/getsecrt/secrt/blob/main/scripts/install.sh to read the same code that secrt.ca/install.sh serves; (b) print the URL prominently in the install script so users who run it once can re-run from the local file; (c) document the audit-first path in the README — fetch the script with -o install.sh, inspect with less, then run with bash. Once installed, secrt update is the maintenance path — install.sh is one-and-done.

subtasks.title: Tests + spec vectors for the update flow
subtasks.description: Coverage targets: (a) update-check module: cache hit, cache miss, network error, GitHub rate-limit (403), invalid JSON, semver comparison edge cases (0.14.10 vs 0.15.0, 0.15.0 vs 0.15.0-rc.1), tag-prefix mismatch, drafts and prereleases skipped. (b) Banner suppression matrix: --silent, --no-update-check, SECRT_NO_UPDATE_CHECK=1, --json, current command is secrt update, stderr is not a TTY. Each as its own table-driven test row. (c) Self-update: SHA-256 mismatch refuses installation, atomic rename works on Unix, the Windows path tries the deferred-rename fallback (gate behind cfg(windows)), --version pinning resolves to the exact release tag. (d) Optional: a CI job that builds a known-good 0.15.0-test artifact, runs secrt update --version 0.15.0-test --install-dir /tmp/test-bin, and asserts the installed binary's --version output matches. Real GitHub API hits should be gated behind the same e2e ignore pattern the existing CLI tests use.

tasks.title: iOS App Clip for secret claiming
tasks.description: Lightweight App Clip target for receiving and decrypting secrets on iOS without installing the full app. Triggered from URLs, QR codes, NFC tags, and iMessage links — the core claim UX (tap link → enter passphrase → see secret) is a perfect fit for the <15MB App Clip format. Prerequisite: a SwiftUI iOS app exists in some form (currently no SwiftUI codebase in the monorepo). Until that lands this task is blocked. Tracking: GitHub issue https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/issues/11.

subtasks.title: Bootstrap a SwiftUI iOS app target (prerequisite)
subtasks.description: An App Clip can only exist as a sub-target of an iOS app. Decide whether to start the iOS app from scratch or share a Rust core via UniFFI. Spec the minimum viable iOS app surface (claim flow only) so the App Clip can be carved out of it.

subtasks.title: Carve the claim flow into a separate App Clip target
subtasks.description: Configure the iOS Xcode project with the App Clip target. Limit the App Clip to the claim/decrypt views — ~15MB cap. Verify no transitive imports pull in unneeded dependencies.

subtasks.title: Configure App Clip URL invocation and Smart Banner support
subtasks.description: Universal Links and the apple-app-site-association file on the secrt.ca and secrt.is hosts. iMessage card configuration. Test invocation from QR, NFC, and Safari Smart Banner.

tasks.title: Unauthenticated secret existence check before claiming
tasks.description: The claim page now shows a confirmation screen ('Someone Sent You a Secret') before consuming the secret. If the secret has already been claimed or expired, the user only finds out after clicking 'View Secret' — the secret is consumed by the claim attempt and the error surfaces too late. Add a lightweight non-consuming endpoint (e.g. HEAD /api/v1/secrets/{id} or a GET that returns a tiny existence/expiry status without requiring the claim token) so the client can pre-flight and show a clear 'unavailable' state if the secret is already gone. Must avoid leaking information beyond what's already implied by a 404 from the claim endpoint. Tracking: GitHub issue https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/issues/25.

subtasks.title: Threat-model the existence check
subtasks.description: Document precisely what an attacker can learn from a non-consuming existence check that they can't already learn from the claim endpoint. Today, claim returns 404 for: not-found, expired, already-claimed, wrong-token. The existence check must not leak strictly more than 'a secret with this ID was created at some point and is currently available to be claimed (or not)'. Decide whether rate-limit shape needs to differ from claim.

subtasks.title: Spec the endpoint in spec/v1/api.md and openapi.yaml
subtasks.description: Decide HEAD vs GET, response shape (likely 200 with empty body if exists+not-expired, 404 otherwise), caching (Cache-Control: no-store), rate limiting (likely the claim limiter or its own bucket).

subtasks.title: Implement on the server (Axum handler + Postgres query)
subtasks.description: A read-only check that the row exists and expires_at > now. No claim_hash verification — that would require a token the client doesn't have. Owner_key must not be returned.

subtasks.title: Wire the web client to call the existence check on the claim page
subtasks.description: On page load, hit the existence endpoint. If 404, render the 'Secret unavailable' state immediately and skip the confirmation screen. If 200, proceed as today. Make sure the call is sent from the secrt origin (not from a third-party referrer that might surface the URL).

tasks.title: Desktop app: custom host configuration for self-hosted instances
tasks.description: Allow the Tauri desktop app to point at a self-hosted secrt server instead of secrt.ca / secrt.is. Settings UI to configure server URL, login/session persistence per-host, and ideally multiple server profiles so a user with both a personal account on secrt.ca and a corporate self-hosted instance can switch between them without re-login. Where possible the Tauri app and the CLI should share config (the CLI already has base_url + api_key in config.toml). Tracking: GitHub issue https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/issues/29.

subtasks.title: Settings UI: server URL field + validation
subtasks.description: Add to the Settings page (also covered by task #37). Validate URL shape, optional 'connection test' button that hits /api/v1/info on the entered host.

subtasks.title: Multi-profile support
subtasks.description: Allow the user to save N labeled profiles (e.g. 'secrt.ca', 'work-self-hosted', 'secrt.is'). Quick-switch UI in the menu bar or a profile picker. Each profile has its own session token and AMK wrapper — keychain entries scoped per profile.

subtasks.title: Share config with the CLI where practical
subtasks.description: Tauri + CLI both have config files. Decide what should be shared (base_url? api_key? passphrase list?) and what stays per-app (window size, etc.). Document the precedence rules.

tasks.title: Desktop app: native file encryption with streaming
tasks.description: Leverage the secrt-core Rust crate directly from Tauri commands instead of running encryption through the WebView's WASM path. Removes the browser memory ceiling for large files, eliminates WASM overhead, and unlocks native UX patterns (drag-and-drop onto the app window or dock icon, native file open/save dialogs, progress indicators for long encryptions). Pair with offline-mode support: encrypt locally and queue the upload for when connectivity returns. Tracking: GitHub issue https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/issues/31.

subtasks.title: Tauri command: native streaming encrypt
subtasks.description: Expose a Tauri command that takes a file path + envelope params and returns a temp ciphertext file path + envelope JSON. Streaming via secrt-core's seal API on a chunked reader. Verify against the existing envelope test vectors.

subtasks.title: Tauri command: native streaming decrypt
subtasks.description: Inverse of the encrypt command. Take a downloaded ciphertext file + url_key (+ optional passphrase) and write decrypted bytes to a user-chosen path. Stream-decrypt via secrt-core's open API.

subtasks.title: Drag-and-drop on the app window and dock icon
subtasks.description: Tauri supports file-drop events on windows. Macos dock-icon drops route through the OS open-with-files event. Wire both to the send flow.

subtasks.title: Batch operations and progress indicators
subtasks.description: Multi-file selection produces a queue. Each item shows progress, success state, and a copyable share link. Cancellable.

subtasks.title: Offline queue: encrypt locally, upload when connectivity returns
subtasks.description: Persist not-yet-uploaded ciphertext on disk (mode 600). On connectivity restore, drain the queue. Show queued items in the UI so users know what's pending.

tasks.title: Fix invalid docs URL in CLI config.toml template
tasks.description: The CLI's config.toml template references https://secrt.ca/docs/config which doesn't resolve. Either point at the README section that documents config, or stand up a real /docs/config page. Lower-stakes fix; visible to every user who runs `secrt config init`. Tracking: GitHub issue https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/issues/41.

subtasks.title: Decide: README anchor or new docs page
subtasks.description: Cheapest is to point at https://github.com/getsecrt/secrt/blob/main/crates/secrt-cli/README.md#configuration (or wherever the CLI README documents config keys). Alternative: create a docs page on secrt.ca and route to it. Decision drives which fix to apply.

subtasks.title: Patch the template in crates/secrt-cli/src/config.rs
subtasks.description: The CONFIG_TEMPLATE constant in config.rs is the source. Update the URL there. Add a tiny test that asserts the template references a non-empty plausible URL.

tasks.title: Sign self-update artifacts with Sigstore/cosign keyless signing
tasks.description:
Closes the supply-chain trust gap acknowledged in spec/v1/cli.md Trust Root and Supply-Chain Notes. Currently, secrt update verifies SHA-256 against a checksum file fetched from the same release - this protects against transport tampering but not against compromise of the GitHub release publishing pipeline. Sigstore/cosign keyless signing closes this: the release-cli.yml workflow signs each artifact and the checksum file with an ephemeral certificate tied to the GitHub Actions OIDC identity of the workflow. Signatures and cert chains are pushed to the public Rekor transparency log. The CLI verifies the signature against the Sigstore trust root plus a workflow identity (the secrt repo's release-cli.yml at a specific tag) before installing.

Why keyless: no long-lived signing key to manage, rotate, or accidentally expose. The trust root is GitHub Actions OIDC plus the public Rekor log.

Scope:
1. Add cosign signing steps to .github/workflows/release-cli.yml. Sign each raw binary, each archive, and the secrt-checksums-sha256.txt file. Push signatures to the corresponding GitHub Release.
2. Add Rust verifier to the secrt CLI's update flow. Use sigstore-rs or shell out to a bundled cosign binary. Verify signature before SHA-256 (transparency log assurance is stronger).
3. Document the verification workflow in the README so users can manually verify a downloaded artifact.
4. Decide policy: refuse install on signature failure (recommended) vs warn-and-continue (weaker but more recoverable). Refuse is the right call for a security-sensitive tool.

References:
- https://docs.sigstore.dev/cosign/keyless/
- https://github.com/sigstore/sigstore-rs
- https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/security-hardening-your-deployments/about-security-hardening-with-openid-connect

Land this PR before secrt has many users.

subtasks.title: Add cosign signing to release-cli.yml
subtasks.description: Sign raw binaries, archives, and the checksum file with cosign in keyless mode. Upload .sig files alongside artifacts.

subtasks.title: Add signature verification to secrt update
subtasks.description: Verify the artifact signature against Sigstore trust root and secrt's GitHub Actions workflow identity before installing. Fail closed on mismatch.

subtasks.title: Document manual verification in README
subtasks.description: Add a short README section showing how a user can manually verify a release artifact with cosign verify-blob.

tasks.title: secrt update: suggest sudo when system-wide install is detected
tasks.description: When `secrt update` hits a permission-denied error and the binary lives in a known system path (e.g. /usr/local/bin, /usr/bin, /opt/*/bin), the error message should mention `sudo secrt update` alongside the existing `--install-dir` suggestion. The 0.16.0 design (`crates/secrt-cli/src/update.rs:1007,1022`) deliberately omitted sudo because the assumed install location was user-space — but for a deliberately root-owned system install, `--install-dir ~/.local/bin` is actively counterproductive: it leaves the old vulnerable binary at /usr/local/bin and creates a parallel install whose precedence depends on PATH ordering. JD hit this on his dev machine running 0.16.1 (binary at /usr/local/bin/secrt, root-owned). Keep `--install-dir` listed first in the message so the least-privilege default still nudges new installs into user-space; add the sudo hint as a second option only when the heuristic confirms a system install.

subtasks.title: Extract message-formatting into a pure function
subtasks.description: Refactor the duplicated permission-denied error string at update.rs:1007 and update.rs:1022 into a single helper, e.g. `fn permission_denied_message(install_dir: &Path, release_url: &str) -> String`. Both call sites then become `writeln!(deps.stderr, "{}", permission_denied_message(...))`. Pure function — no IO, fully unit-testable.

subtasks.title: Add system-install heuristic
subtasks.description: Add `fn looks_like_system_install(install_dir: &Path) -> bool`. True when `install_dir` is one of: `/usr/local/bin`, `/usr/bin`, `/usr/local/sbin`, `/usr/sbin`, or matches `/opt/*/bin`. False otherwise (including ~/ anything, /tmp, cargo bin, brew Cellar, etc — those are either caught by managed-install classification earlier or genuinely user-space). Unix-only behavior; on Windows the heuristic is always false (no privilege-elevation hint there — Windows installs are typically per-user or via installer/MSI).

subtasks.title: Wire heuristic into the message
subtasks.description: When `looks_like_system_install(install_dir)` is true, the message gains a third line / inline alternative: `or, if you installed system-wide: sudo secrt update`. Keep `--install-dir` first to preserve the user-space default. Manual download stays as fallback. Phrasing must avoid making sudo look like the recommended path.

subtasks.title: Tests
subtasks.description: Two test groups, both pure-function (no IO, no root): (a) `looks_like_system_install` table-driven across `/usr/local/bin`, `/usr/bin`, `/opt/secrt/bin`, `~/.local/bin`, `~/.cargo/bin`, `/tmp/foo`, on Windows always false. (b) `permission_denied_message` with two cases: user-writable path (current message, no sudo), and system path (message includes the `sudo secrt update` line and still mentions `--install-dir`). Co-locate with the existing update.rs tests near line 1345.

subtasks.title: Spec touch-up (optional)
subtasks.description: If `spec/v1/cli.md` documents the permission-denied behavior verbatim (it might, given the 0.16.0 update spec section), update the spec to note the new conditional sudo hint. If the spec only documents exit codes, no change needed.
